machine_state_at_capture.background_workload: Mistral 7B HuggingFace download (~2.3 MB/s, ~1.5 GB done)
machine_state_at_capture.note: These conditions intentionally include the worst case noise so the floor is conservative. Re-run after `bash bench/setup_perf.sh` for paper-grade noise floor (will be ~10x lower for sub-ns ops).

ci_width_ns_explanation: 2 × 359 ps cross-run drift on noop_u64 (the dominant noise source on this machine without setup_perf.sh). Sub-ns benchmarks below this delta are statistical noise per METHODOLOGY.md §6 reporting rule.

noop_u64.description: Single black_box(0u64) call — measures pure criterion + scheduler overhead
noop_u64.noise_floor_explanation: 2 × cross_run_drift = 2 × 360 ps = 0.720 ns. Anything smaller is noise.

noop_arith.description: 3 black_box ops + wrapping_mul — measures ~3-instruction sequence overhead
noop_arith.noise_floor_explanation: 2 × 2.8 ps cross-run drift = 0.0056 ns. Multi-instruction benches amortize overhead and are 100× more stable than single-instruction benches.

key_findings: Run 4 shows a +64.671% jump on noop_u64 (557 ps → 916 ps) — exactly the kind of HWP / governor frequency drift that METHODOLOGY.md §3 warns about. CPU max freq is 5.6 GHz but powersave + HWP + Mistral download in background caused mid-bench frequency change.
noop_arith remained stable across all 5 runs (max variance 0.2%). Multi-instruction sequences amortize the per-iteration scheduler overhead and are 100× more stable than single-instruction sequences.
Within-run CI half-widths are tiny (~0.5 ps for noop_u64, ~1.4 ps for noop_arith). Criterion's outlier filtering is doing its job within a single run.
Cross-run drift is the dominant noise source under default (powersave) conditions. This is unmeasurable from a single criterion run — only multi-run capture reveals it.
Outliers ranged 6-14% per run, well above criterion's default 'severe outlier' threshold. This is expected with background load (Mistral download) and confirms the need for setup_perf.sh.

reporting_rule.rule: Per bench/METHODOLOGY.md §6, any benchmark improvement smaller than 2× the noise floor must be reported as 'below noise floor', not as a number.

sub_nanosecond_ops.implication: Any FajarQuant micro-op claim of 'X ns improvement' where X < 1.44 ns is below the noise floor under current (non-paper-grade) machine state.

multi_instruction_ops.implication: Multi-instruction micro-ops can claim improvements as small as 0.011 ns (11 ps).

recommendations: Re-run noise_floor.rs after `bash bench/setup_perf.sh` (V26 §C2.0.4 — committed in b6a0bb1) to capture paper-grade noise floor. Expected ~10x improvement for noop_u64 (governor=performance pins frequency, eliminates the cross-run drift seen in runs 4-5 here).
Capture a second noise_floor.json named noise_floor_paper_grade.json after setup_perf.sh apply, and cite that in the paper instead of this one.

For C2.1+ benchmarks (latency, throughput, memory), ensure each measurement is well above the multi-instruction floor (0.0112 ns). The FajarQuant quantize_layer operations are O(microseconds), so they're far above the floor — paper-grade noise is not a concern for those.
Add a CI smoke test that runs `cargo bench --bench noise_floor` once per push and asserts cross-run drift < 50% as a regression sentry.
rerun_command: cd ~/Documents/fajarquant && for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do cargo bench --bench noise_floor; done | grep -E 'time:|change:|outliers'
